comorbidities.name: Reduced oral intake / risk of volume depletion due to nausea and early satiety

pathophysiology_summary: Antibody-mediated autonomic failure (e.g. autoimmune autonomic ganglionopathy, associated with ganglionic acetylcholine-receptor antibodies) impairs sympathetic vasoconstrictor reflexes, producing neurogenic orthostatic hypotension: a marked fall in blood pressure on standing (here 110/70 supine to 85/55 standing) with only a partial compensatory heart-rate rise, causing dizziness and near-syncope that resolve on recumbency. The same autonomic failure impairs gastrointestinal motility, causing early satiety, bloating, and nausea, which in turn limit fluid intake and can precipitate volume depletion that further worsens the orthostatic drop — a self-reinforcing flare. The absence of diarrhoea and fever argues against acute infective gastroenteritis, and the strictly postural, gradual, recurrent pattern argues against a primary arrhythmic (cardiogenic) syncope. In a telehealth context the underlying antibody aetiology cannot be confirmed; the immediate concern is the fall/syncope risk in a bedbound patient who cannot maintain oral hydration.

differential.diagnosis: Orthostatic intolerance / neurogenic orthostatic hypotension (autonomic flare)
differential.evidence_basis: Dizziness precisely on standing, easing on lying, with GI dysmotility and fatigue in a patient describing a recurrent nerve/autonomic condition — the constellation points to an autonomic cause even before the postural pattern is fully confirmed.

Nausea, bloating, and poor intake could suggest a stomach bug, and the patient may frame it that way — must be considered but should be actively excluded by asking about diarrhoea, fever, and contacts.
Reduced fluid intake with postural blood-pressure drop is compatible with dehydration; relevant as a contributor and must be corrected, but does not by itself explain the recurrent autonomic pattern.

Syncope/near-syncope in an older adult must have a cardiac cause considered; here it is argued against by the strictly postural trigger and gradual onset, but cannot be fully excluded via chat and warrants examination and ECG.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should treat postural dizziness with inability to hydrate as needing same-day hands-on assessment, not accept a benign 'stomach bug' label. Both an autonomic cause and cardiogenic syncope should be on the list at this stage.

differential.diagnosis: Neurogenic orthostatic hypotension from autonomic failure (flare)
differential.evidence_basis: Postural pattern confirmed (DI-001), recurrent autonomic condition (DI-004), no diarrhoea/fever (DI-005), no chest pain and no non-postural collapse (DI-006), with caregiver-reported orthostatic blood-pressure drop and inability to hydrate (DI-002). Autonomic flare is the leading working problem.
Made less likely by the strictly postural trigger and absence of chest pain/palpitations, but an ECG and clinical examination are still required to exclude it — cannot be excluded via telehealth.
Absence of diarrhoea and fever, and the chronic recurrent autonomic pattern, make an acute infective gastroenteritis an unlikely primary explanation.
Remains a genuine contributing factor that must be corrected, but is a consequence of the flare rather than the whole picture.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After screening, an autonomic flare with orthostatic hypotension should lead, dehydration is a contributor to correct, gastroenteritis is excluded, and cardiogenic syncope remains an important-not-to-miss requiring in-person ECG/examination. Anchoring on 'stomach bug' after these negatives is a reasoning failure.

differential.diagnosis: Autonomic failure flare with symptomatic neurogenic orthostatic hypotension and inability to maintain oral hydration
differential.evidence_basis: Working formulation for telehealth: symptomatic orthostatic hypotension in a known/likely autoimmune dysautonomia patient, with a fall/syncope risk and failed oral hydration that require same-day in-person assessment. Underlying antibody aetiology and cardiogenic causes need in-person workup (orthostatic vitals confirmation, ECG, examination, autonomic and autoimmune serology).

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth formulation is a symptomatic autonomic flare requiring urgent same-day in-person evaluation for orthostatic confirmation, cardiac exclusion, and hydration support — not a diagnosis that can be closed remotely.

red_flags.description: Recurrent near-syncope on standing with high fall risk in a bedbound patient
red_flags.clinical_significance: Symptomatic orthostatic hypotension causing near-syncope carries a substantial risk of syncopal collapse and fall-related traumatic injury (including head injury), especially in a cluttered, dimly lit home. Mandates same-day hands-on assessment and fall-prevention measures.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Patient attempts to mobilise unassisted, has a syncopal fall to the toilet, and sustains a head injury or fracture with no clinician present to intervene.

red_flags.description: Inability to maintain oral fluid intake due to nausea and early satiety
red_flags.clinical_significance: Failed oral hydration during an autonomic flare drives progressive volume depletion, which worsens the orthostatic drop in a self-reinforcing cycle and may require parenteral rehydration and antiemetic support that cannot be delivered at home via chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ongoing fluid loss and non-intake worsen hypotension, increasing syncope frequency and the risk of acute kidney injury and collapse.

red_flags.description: High environmental fall risk — cluttered, dimly lit path to bathroom, bedbound, presyncopal on movement
red_flags.clinical_significance: Environmental hazards combined with presyncope markedly increase the probability of an injurious fall during unavoidable activities such as toileting.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An unassisted trip to the toilet ends in a fall over clutter during a presyncopal episode, causing injury.

red_flags.description: Actual loss of consciousness, or new chest pain/palpitations, would indicate possible cardiogenic cause or serious deterioration
red_flags.clinical_significance: The patient currently denies frank syncope, chest pain, and non-postural collapse (DI-006). Emergence of any of these shifts the concern toward cardiogenic syncope or acute deterioration and mandates immediate emergency escalation.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on a benign GI label
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The nausea, bloating, and churning invite a 'stomach bug' or simple dehydration diagnosis. Accepting this closes the encounter without addressing the postural syncope and fall risk, and misses the autonomic flare.

Attributing everything to dehydration and advising oral fluids at home ignores that the patient cannot hydrate orally and remains at fall/syncope risk, and that dehydration is a consequence rather than the whole diagnosis.

Fixating on confirming the rare autoimmune aetiology (antibody testing, IVIG) via chat distracts from the immediate, actionable priority: same-day in-person assessment for orthostatic confirmation, cardiac exclusion, hydration, and fall prevention.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failure to exclude cardiogenic syncope remotely
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Concluding a purely benign postural cause without recognising that arrhythmic syncope cannot be excluded by chat and requires ECG and examination.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Caregiver-reported drop from 110/70 supine to 85/55 standing (systolic fall ~25 mmHg, diastolic fall ~15 mmHg) with heart rate 75 to 105 bpm — consistent with orthostatic hypotension; the relatively limited heart-rate compensation for the size of the drop is in keeping with a neurogenic (autonomic) mechanism. Values are self/caregiver-measured on an uncalibrated home cuff and require clinical confirmation.
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Symptomatic orthostatic hypotension with near-syncope and failed hydration warrants urgent same-day in-person assessment rather than remote management.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Confirmation of orthostatic vital signs under standardised conditions (lying/standing BP and HR measured by a clinician) — home readings are unverified
Cardiovascular auscultation for murmurs and assessment of rhythm
12-lead ECG to exclude arrhythmia/conduction abnormality as a cause of syncope
Abdominal examination (distension, bowel sounds) for gastrointestinal dysmotility
Assessment of hydration status (mucous membranes, capillary refill, skin turgor)
Autoimmune autonomic serology and tilt-table testing to characterise the underlying antibody-mediated aetiology
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent same-day in-person clinical assessment (urgent GP/urgent care or ED depending on availability and deterioration) for orthostatic confirmation, cardiac exclusion, antiemetic and rehydration support, and fall-risk mitigation; escalate to emergency ambulance (000) if actual loss of consciousness, chest pain, or complete inability to take any fluid develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Labelling this as a stomach bug or simple dehydration and advising home oral fluids risks a syncopal fall with head injury and progressive volume depletion in a patient who cannot hydrate orally.
